definitions.name: signInWithIdToken
definitions.description:
Allows signing in with an OIDC ID token. The authentication provider used
should be enabled and configured.
remarks:
- Use an ID token to sign in.
- Especially useful when implementing sign in using native platform dialogs in mobile or desktop apps using Sign in with Apple or Sign in with Google on iOS and Android.
- You can also use Google's [One Tap](https://developers.google.com/identity/gsi/web/guides/display-google-one-tap) and [Automatic sign-in](https://developers.google.com/identity/gsi/web/guides/automatic-sign-in-sign-out) via this API.

properties.name: access_token
properties.description: If the ID token contains an `at_hash` claim, then the hash of this value is compared to the value in the ID token.

properties.name: nonce
properties.description: If the ID token contains a `nonce` claim, then the hash of this value is compared to the value in the ID token.

properties.name: captchaToken
properties.description: Verification token received when the user completes the captcha on the site.

properties.name: provider
properties.description: Provider name or OIDC `iss` value identifying which provider should be used to verify the provided token. Supported names: `google`, `apple`, `azure`, `facebook`, `kakao`. Use the `custom:` prefix for custom OIDC providers (e.g. `custom:my-oidc-provider`).

properties.name: token
properties.description: OIDC ID token issued by the specified provider. The `iss` claim in the ID token must match the supplied provider. Some ID tokens contain an `at_hash` which require that you provide an `access_token` value to be accepted properly. If the token contains a `nonce` claim you must supply the nonce used to obtain the ID token.

definitions.name: signInWithOAuth
definitions.description:
Log in an existing user via a third-party provider.
This method supports the PKCE flow.
remarks:
- This method is used for signing in using [Social Login (OAuth) providers](/docs/guides/auth#configure-third-party-providers).
- It works by redirecting your application to the provider's authorization screen, before bringing back the user to your app.

properties.name: queryParams
properties.description: An object of query params

properties.name: redirectTo
properties.description: A URL to send the user to after they are confirmed.

properties.name: scopes
properties.description: A space-separated list of scopes granted to the OAuth application.

properties.name: skipBrowserRedirect
properties.description: If set to true does not immediately redirect the current browser context to visit the OAuth authorization page for the provider.

properties.name: provider
properties.description: One of the providers supported by GoTrue.

properties.name: code
properties.description:
Error code associated with the error. Most errors coming from
HTTP responses will have a code, though some errors that occur
before a response is received will not have one present. In that
case #status will also be undefined.

properties.name: status
properties.description: HTTP status code that caused the error.

examples.title: Sign in using a third-party provider
examples.notes:
parameter. This parameter defaults to the [](/docs/guides/auth/redirect-urls#use-wildcards-in-redirect-urls). It does not redirect the user immediately after invoking this method.
- See [redirect URLs and wildcards](/docs/guides/auth/redirect-urls#use-wildcards-in-redirect-urls) to add additional redirect URLs to your project.

examples.title: Sign in using a third-party provider with redirect
examples.notes:
parameter. This parameter defaults to the [](/docs/guides/auth/redirect-urls#use-wildcards-in-redirect-urls). It does not redirect the user immediately after invoking this method.
- See [redirect URLs and wildcards](/docs/guides/auth/redirect-urls#use-wildcards-in-redirect-urls) to add additional redirect URLs to your project.

examples.title: Sign in with scopes and access provider tokens
examples.notes:
option takes in **a space-separated list** of scopes.

Because OAuth sign-in often includes redirects, you should register an  callback immediately after you create the Supabase client. This callback will listen for the presence of  and  properties on the  object and store them in local storage. The client library will emit these values **only once** immediately after the user signs in. You can then access them by looking them up in local storage, or send them to your backend servers for further processing.

Finally, make sure you remove them from local storage on the  event. If the OAuth provider supports token revocation, make sure you call those APIs either from the frontend or schedule them to be called on the backend.

definitions.name: signInWithOtp
definitions.description:
Log in a user using magiclink or a one-time password (OTP).

If the `{{ .ConfirmationURL }}` variable is specified in the email template, a magiclink will be sent.
If the `{{ .Token }}` variable is specified in the email template, an OTP will be sent.
If you're using phone sign-ins, only an OTP will be sent. You won't be able to send a magiclink for phone sign-ins.

Be aware that you may get back an error message that will not distinguish
between the cases where the account does not exist or, that the account
can only be accessed via social login.

Do note that you will need to configure a Whatsapp sender on Twilio
if you are using phone sign in with the 'whatsapp' channel. The whatsapp
channel is not supported on other providers
at this time.
This method supports PKCE when an email is passed.
remarks:
- Requires either an email or phone number.
- This method is used for passwordless sign-ins where a OTP is sent to the user's email or phone number.
- If the user doesn't exist, `signInWithOtp()` will signup the user instead. To restrict this behavior, you can set `shouldCreateUser` in `SignInWithPasswordlessCredentials.options` to `false`.
- If you're using an email, you can configure whether you want the user to receive a magiclink or a OTP.
- If you're using phone, you can configure whether you want the user to receive a OTP.
- The magic link's destination URL is determined by the [`SITE_URL`](/docs/guides/auth/redirect-urls#use-wildcards-in-redirect-urls).
- See [redirect URLs and wildcards](/docs/guides/auth/redirect-urls#use-wildcards-in-redirect-urls) to add additional redirect URLs to your project.
- Magic links and OTPs share the same implementation. To send users a one-time code instead of a magic link, [modify the magic link email template](/dashboard/project/_/auth/templates) to include `{{ .Token }}` instead of `{{ .ConfirmationURL }}`.
- See our [Twilio Phone Auth Guide](/docs/guides/auth/phone-login?showSMSProvider=Twilio) for details about configuring WhatsApp sign in.

definitions.name: signInWithSSO
definitions.description:
Attempts a single-sign on using an enterprise Identity Provider. A
successful SSO attempt will redirect the current page to the identity
provider authorization page. The redirect URL is implementation and SSO
protocol specific.

You can use it by providing a SSO domain. Typically you can extract this
domain by asking users for their email address. If this domain is
registered on the Auth instance the redirect will use that organization's
currently active SSO Identity Provider for the login.

If you have built an organization-specific login page, you can use the
organization's SSO Identity Provider UUID directly instead.
remarks:
- Before you can call this method you need to [establish a connection](/docs/guides/auth/sso/auth-sso-saml#managing-saml-20-connections) to an identity provider. Use the [CLI commands](/docs/reference/cli/supabase-sso) to do this.
- If you've associated an email domain to the identity provider, you can use the `domain` property to start a sign-in flow.
- In case you need to use a different way to start the authentication flow with an identity provider, you can use the `providerId` property. For example:
    - Mapping specific user email addresses with an identity provider.
    - Using different hints to identity the identity provider to be used by the user, like a company-specific page, IP address or other tracking information.

definitions.name: signInWithWeb3
definitions.description:
Signs in a user by verifying a message signed by the user's private key.
Supports Ethereum (via Sign-In-With-Ethereum) & Solana (Sign-In-With-Solana) standards,
both of which derive from the EIP-4361 standard
With slight variation on Solana's side.
remarks:
- Uses a Web3 (Ethereum, Solana) wallet to sign a user in.
- Read up on the [potential for abuse](/docs/guides/auth/auth-web3#potential-for-abuse) before using it.

definitions.name: signOut
definitions.description:
Inside a browser context, `signOut()` will remove the logged in user from the browser session and log them out - removing all items from localstorage and then trigger a `"SIGNED_OUT"` event.

For server-side management, you can revoke all refresh tokens for a user by passing a user's JWT through to `auth.api.signOut(JWT: string)`.
There is no way to revoke a user's access token jwt until it expires. It is recommended to set a shorter expiry on the jwt for this reason.

If using `others` scope, no `SIGNED_OUT` event is fired!
remarks:
- In order to use the `signOut()` method, the user needs to be signed in first.
- By default, `signOut()` uses the global scope, which signs out all other sessions that the user is logged into as well. Customize this behavior by passing a scope parameter.
- Since Supabase Auth uses JWTs for authentication, the access token JWT will be valid until it's expired. When the user signs out, Supabase revokes the refresh token and deletes the JWT from the client-side. This does not revoke the JWT and it will still be valid until it expires.

properties.name: scope
properties.description:
Determines which sessions should be
logged out. Global means all
sessions by this account. Local
means only this session. Others
means all other sessions except the
current one. When using others,
there is no sign-out event fired on
the current session!

properties.name: code
properties.description:
Error code associated with the error. Most errors coming from
HTTP responses will have a code, though some errors that occur
before a response is received will not have one present. In that
case #status will also be undefined.

properties.name: status
properties.description: HTTP status code that caused the error.

examples.title: Sign out (all sessions)
examples.code: const { error } = await supabase.auth.signOut()

examples.title: Sign out (current session)
examples.code: const { error } = await supabase.auth.signOut({ scope: 'local' })

examples.title: Sign out (other sessions)
examples.code: const { error } = await supabase.auth.signOut({ scope: 'others' })

definitions.name: signUp
definitions.description:
Creates a new user.

Be aware that if a user account exists in the system you may get back an
error message that attempts to hide this information from the user.
This method has support for PKCE via email signups. The PKCE flow cannot be used when autoconfirm is enabled.
remarks:
- By default, the user needs to verify their email address before logging in. To turn this off, disable **Confirm email** in [your project](/dashboard/project/_/auth/providers).
- **Confirm email** determines if users need to confirm their email address after signing up.
  - If **Confirm email** is enabled, a `user` is returned but `session` is null.
  - If **Confirm email** is disabled, both a `user` and a `session` are returned.
- When the user confirms their email address, they are redirected to the [`SITE_URL`](/docs/guides/auth/redirect-urls#use-wildcards-in-redirect-urls) by default. You can modify your `SITE_URL` or add additional redirect URLs in [your project](/dashboard/project/_/auth/url-configuration).
- If signUp() is called for an existing confirmed user:
  - When both **Confirm email** and **Confirm phone** (even when phone provider is disabled) are enabled in [your project](/dashboard/project/_/auth/providers), an obfuscated/fake user object is returned.
  - When either **Confirm email** or **Confirm phone** (even when phone provider is disabled) is disabled, the error message, `User already registered` is returned.
- To fetch the currently logged-in user, refer to [`getUser()`](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-getuser).

definitions.name: startAutoRefresh
definitions.description:
Starts an auto-refresh process in the background. The session is checked
every few seconds. Close to the time of expiration a process is started to
refresh the session. If refreshing fails it will be retried for as long as
necessary.

If you set the GoTrueClientOptions#autoRefreshToken you don't need
to call this function, it will be called for you.

On browsers the refresh process works only when the tab/window is in the
foreground to conserve resources as well as prevent race conditions and
flooding auth with requests. If you call this method any managed
visibility change callback will be removed and you must manage visibility
changes on your own.

On non-browser platforms the refresh process works *continuously* in the
background, which may not be desirable. You should hook into your
platform's foreground indication mechanism and call these methods
appropriately to conserve resources.

#stopAutoRefresh
remarks:
- Only useful in non-browser environments such as React Native or Electron.
- The Supabase Auth library automatically starts and stops proactively refreshing the session when a tab is focused or not.
- On non-browser platforms, such as mobile or desktop apps built with web technologies, the library is not able to effectively determine whether the application is _focused_ or not.
- To give this hint to the application, you should be calling this method when the app is in focus and calling `supabase.auth.stopAutoRefresh()` when it's out of focus.

examples.title: Start and stop auto refresh in React Native

definitions.name: stopAutoRefresh
definitions.description:
Stops an active auto refresh process running in the background (if any).

If you call this method any managed visibility change callback will be
removed and you must manage visibility changes on your own.

See #startAutoRefresh for more details.
remarks:
- Only useful in non-browser environments such as React Native or Electron.
- The Supabase Auth library automatically starts and stops proactively refreshing the session when a tab is focused or not.
- On non-browser platforms, such as mobile or desktop apps built with web technologies, the library is not able to effectively determine whether the application is _focused_ or not.
- When your application goes in the background or out of focus, call this method to stop the proactive refreshing of the session.

examples.title: Start and stop auto refresh in React Native

definitions.name: unlinkIdentity
definitions.description: Unlinks an identity from a user by deleting it. The user will no longer be able to sign in with that identity once it's unlinked.
remarks:
- The **Enable Manual Linking** option must be enabled from your [project's authentication settings](/dashboard/project/_/auth/providers).
- The user needs to be signed in to call `unlinkIdentity()`.
- The user must have at least 2 identities in order to unlink an identity.
- The identity to be unlinked must belong to the user.

definitions.name: verifyOtp
definitions.description: Log in a user given a User supplied OTP or TokenHash received through mobile or email.
remarks:
- The `verifyOtp` method takes in different verification types.
- If a phone number is used, the type can either be:
  1. `sms` – Used when verifying a one-time password (OTP) sent via SMS during sign-up or sign-in.
  2. `phone_change` – Used when verifying an OTP sent to a new phone number during a phone number update process.
- If an email address is used, the type can be one of the following (note: `signup` and `magiclink` types are deprecated):
  1. `email` – Used when verifying an OTP sent to the user's email during sign-up or sign-in.
  2. `recovery` – Used when verifying an OTP sent for account recovery, typically after a password reset request.
  3. `invite` – Used when verifying an OTP sent as part of an invitation to join a project or organization.
  4. `email_change` – Used when verifying an OTP sent to a new email address during an email update process.
- The verification type used should be determined based on the corresponding auth method called before `verifyOtp` to sign up / sign-in a user.
- The `TokenHash` is contained in the [email templates](/docs/guides/auth/auth-email-templates) and can be used to sign in.  You may wish to use the hash for the PKCE flow for Server Side Auth. Read [the Password-based Auth guide](/docs/guides/auth/passwords) for more details.

definitions.name: approveAuthorization
definitions.description:
Approves an OAuth authorization request.
Only relevant when the OAuth 2.1 server is enabled in Supabase Auth.

After approval, the user's consent is stored and an authorization code is generated.
The response contains a complete redirect URL with the authorization code and state.

parameters.name: authorizationId
parameters.description: The authorization ID to approve

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Optional parameters

properties.name: skipBrowserRedirect
properties.description: If false (default), automatically redirects the browser to the OAuth client. If true, returns the redirect_url without automatic redirect (useful for custom handling).

properties.name: redirect_url
properties.description: Complete redirect URL with authorization code and state parameters (e.g., "https://app.com/callback?code=xxx&state=yyy")

definitions.name: denyAuthorization
definitions.description:
Denies an OAuth authorization request.
Only relevant when the OAuth 2.1 server is enabled in Supabase Auth.

After denial, the response contains a redirect URL with an OAuth error
(access_denied) to inform the OAuth client that the user rejected the request.

definitions.name: challenge
definitions.description:
Prepares a challenge used to verify that a user has access to a MFA
factor.
remarks:
- An [enrolled factor](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-enroll) is required before creating a challenge.
- To verify a challenge, see [`mfa.verify()`](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-verify).
- A phone factor sends a code to the user upon challenge. The channel defaults to `sms` unless otherwise specified.

properties.name: factorId
properties.description: ID of the factor to be challenged. Returned in enroll().

properties.name: code
properties.description:
Error code associated with the error. Most errors coming from
HTTP responses will have a code, though some errors that occur
before a response is received will not have one present. In that
case #status will also be undefined.

properties.name: status
properties.description: HTTP status code that caused the error.

properties.name: expires_at
properties.description: Timestamp in UNIX seconds when this challenge will no longer be usable.

properties.name: id
properties.description: ID of the newly created challenge.

properties.name: type
properties.description: Factor Type which generated the challenge

Create a challenge for a phone factor
Create a challenge for a phone factor (WhatsApp)

definitions.name: challengeAndVerify
definitions.description:
Helper method which creates a challenge and immediately uses the given code to verify against it thereafter. The verification code is
provided by the user by entering a code seen in their authenticator app.
remarks:
- Intended for use with only TOTP factors.
- An [enrolled factor](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-enroll) is required before invoking `challengeAndVerify()`.
- Executes [`mfa.challenge()`](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-challenge) and [`mfa.verify()`](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-verify) in a single step.

definitions.name: enroll
definitions.description:
Starts the enrollment process for a new Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
factor. This method creates a new `unverified` factor.
To verify a factor, present the QR code or secret to the user and ask them to add it to their
authenticator app.
The user has to enter the code from their authenticator app to verify it.

Upon verifying a factor, all other sessions are logged out and the current session's authenticator level is promoted to `aal2`.
remarks:
- Use `totp` or `phone` as the `factorType` and use the returned `id` to create a challenge.
- To create a challenge, see [`mfa.challenge()`](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-challenge).
- To verify a challenge, see [`mfa.verify()`](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-verify).
- To create and verify a TOTP challenge in a single step, see [`mfa.challengeAndVerify()`](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-challengeandverify).
- To generate a QR code for the `totp` secret in Next.js, you can do the following:
```html
<Image src={data.totp.qr_code} alt={data.totp.uri} layout="fill"></Image>
```
- The `challenge` and `verify` steps are separated when using Phone factors as the user will need time to receive and input the code obtained from the SMS in challenge.

properties.name: factorType
properties.description: The type of factor being enrolled.

properties.name: friendlyName
properties.description: Human readable name assigned to the factor.

properties.name: issuer
properties.description: Domain which the user is enrolled with.

examples.title: Enroll a time-based, one-time password (TOTP) factor
Enroll a Phone Factor

definitions.name: getAuthenticatorAssuranceLevel
definitions.description:
Returns the Authenticator Assurance Level (AAL) for the active session.

- `aal1` (or `null`) means that the user's identity has been verified only
with a conventional login (email+password, OTP, magic link, social login,
etc.).
- `aal2` means that the user's identity has been verified both with a conventional login and at least one MFA factor.

When called without a JWT parameter, this method is fairly quick (microseconds)
and rarely uses the network. When a JWT is provided (useful in server-side
environments like Edge Functions where no session is stored), this method
will make a network request to validate the user and fetch their MFA factors.
remarks:
- Authenticator Assurance Level (AAL) is the measure of the strength of an authentication mechanism.
- In Supabase, having an AAL of `aal1` refers to having the 1st factor of authentication such as an email and password or OAuth sign-in while `aal2` refers to the 2nd factor of authentication such as a time-based, one-time-password (TOTP) or Phone factor.
- If the user has a verified factor, the `nextLevel` field will return `aal2`, else, it will return `aal1`.
- An optional `jwt` parameter can be passed to check the AAL level of a specific JWT instead of the current session.

parameters.name: jwt
parameters.description: Takes in an optional access token JWT. If no JWT is provided, the JWT from the current session is used.

properties.name: currentAuthenticationMethods
properties.description:
A list of all authentication methods attached to this session. Use
the information here to detect the last time a user verified a
factor, for example if implementing a step-up scenario.

Supports both RFC-8176 compliant format (string[]) and detailed format (AMREntry[]).
- String format: ['password', 'otp'] - RFC-8176 compliant
- Object format: [{ method: 'password', timestamp: 1234567890 }] - includes timestamps

properties.name: method
properties.description: Authentication method name.

properties.name: timestamp
properties.description:
Timestamp when the method was successfully used. Represents number of
seconds since 1st January 1970 (UNIX epoch) in UTC.

properties.name: currentLevel
properties.description: Current AAL level of the session.

properties.name: nextLevel
properties.description:
Next possible AAL level for the session. If the next level is higher
than the current one, the user should go through MFA.

examples.title: Get the AAL details of a session
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase.auth.mfa.getAuthenticatorAssuranceLevel()
const { currentLevel, nextLevel, currentAuthenticationMethods } = data

examples.title: Get the AAL details for a specific JWT
examples.code: const { data, error } = await supabase.auth.mfa.getAuthenticatorAssuranceLevel(jwt)

definitions.name: listFactors
definitions.description: Returns the list of MFA factors enabled for this user.

properties.name: all
properties.description: All available factors (verified and unverified).

definitions.name: unenroll
definitions.description:
Unenroll removes a MFA factor.
A user has to have an `aal2` authenticator level in order to unenroll a `verified` factor.

properties.name: factorId
properties.description: ID of the factor being unenrolled.

properties.name: id
properties.description: ID of the factor that was successfully unenrolled.

examples.title: Unenroll a factor

definitions.name: verify
definitions.description:
Verifies a code against a challenge. The verification code is
provided by the user by entering a code seen in their authenticator app.
remarks: - To verify a challenge, please [create a challenge](/docs/reference/javascript/auth-mfa-challenge) first.

properties.name: challengeId
properties.description: ID of the challenge being verified. Returned in challenge().

properties.name: code
properties.description: Verification code provided by the user.

properties.name: factorId
properties.description: ID of the factor being verified. Returned in enroll().

properties.name: access_token
properties.description: New access token (JWT) after successful verification.

properties.name: expires_in
properties.description: Number of seconds in which the access token will expire.

properties.name: refresh_token
properties.description: Refresh token you can use to obtain new access tokens when expired.

properties.name: token_type
properties.description: Type of token, always `bearer`.

properties.name: user
properties.description: Updated user profile.

properties.name: provider
properties.description: The first provider that the user used to sign up with.

properties.name: providers
properties.description: A list of all providers that the user has linked to their account.

properties.name: factor_type
properties.description: Type of factor. `totp` and `phone` supported with this version

properties.name: friendly_name
properties.description: Friendly name of the factor, useful to disambiguate between multiple factors.

properties.name: id
properties.description: ID of the factor.

properties.name: status
properties.description: The verification status of the factor, default is `unverified` after `.enroll()`, then `verified` after the user verifies it with `.verify()`

properties.name: factor_type
properties.description: Type of factor. `totp` and `phone` supported with this version

properties.name: friendly_name
properties.description: Friendly name of the factor, useful to disambiguate between multiple factors.

properties.name: id
properties.description: ID of the factor.

properties.name: status
properties.description: The verification status of the factor, default is `unverified` after `.enroll()`, then `verified` after the user verifies it with `.verify()`

definitions.name: constructor
definitions.description: Creates a builder configured for a specific PostgREST request.

examples.title: Example 1
Creating a Postgrest query builder

definitions.name: overrideTypes
definitions.description: Override the type of the returned `data` field in the response.

examples.title: Example 1
examples.code:
// Merge with existing types (default behavior)
const query = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ custom_field: string }>()

// Replace existing types completely
const replaceQuery = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ id: number; name: string }, { merge: false }>()

examples.title: Complete Override type of successful response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<Array<MyType>, { merge: false }>()

examples.title: Complete Override type of object response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .maybeSingle()
  .overrideTypes<MyType, { merge: false }>()
examples.response: let x: typeof data // MyType | null

examples.title: Partial Override type of successful response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<Array<{ status: "A" | "B" }>>()
examples.response: let x: typeof data // Array<CountryRowProperties & { status: "A" | "B" }>

examples.title: Partial Override type of object response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .maybeSingle()
  .overrideTypes<{ status: "A" | "B" }>()
examples.response: let x: typeof data // CountryRowProperties & { status: "A" | "B" } | null

examples.title: Example 5
examples.code:
// Merge with existing types (default behavior)
const query = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ custom_field: string }>()

// Replace existing types completely
const replaceQuery = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ id: number; name: string }, { merge: false }>()

definitions.name: returns
definitions.description: Override the type of the returned `data`.

definitions.name: setHeader
definitions.description: Set an HTTP header for the request.

definitions.name: throwOnError
definitions.description:
If there's an error with the query, throwOnError will reject the promise by
throwing the error instead of returning it as part of a successful response.

https://github.com/supabase/supabase-js/issues/92

definitions.name: constructor
definitions.description: Creates a PostgREST client.
remarks:
- A `timeout` option (in milliseconds) can be set to automatically abort requests that take too long.
- A `urlLengthLimit` option (default: 8000) can be set to control when URL length warnings are included in error messages for aborted requests.

parameters.name: url
parameters.description: URL of the PostgREST endpoint

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Named parameters

properties.name: fetch
properties.description: Custom fetch

properties.name: headers
properties.description: Custom headers

properties.name: schema
properties.description: Postgres schema to switch to

properties.name: timeout
properties.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds for all requests. When set, requests will automatically abort after this duration to prevent indefinite hangs.

properties.name: urlLengthLimit
properties.description: Maximum URL length in characters before warnings/errors are triggered. Defaults to 8000.

properties.name: from
properties.description: Perform a query on a table or a view.

examples.title: Example 1
Creating a Postgrest client
With timeout

definitions.name: from
definitions.description: Perform a query on a table or a view.

properties.name: insert
properties.description:
Perform an INSERT into the table or view.

By default, inserted rows are not returned. To return it, chain the call
with `.select()`.

properties.name: upsert
properties.description:
Perform an UPSERT on the table or view. Depending on the column(s) passed
to `onConflict`, `.upsert()` allows you to perform the equivalent of
`.insert()` if a row with the corresponding `onConflict` columns doesn't
exist, or if it does exist, perform an alternative action depending on
`ignoreDuplicates`.

By default, upserted rows are not returned. To return it, chain the call
with `.select()`.

definitions.name: rpc
definitions.description: Perform a function call.

parameters.name: fn
parameters.description: The function name to call

parameters.name: args
parameters.description: The arguments to pass to the function call

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Named parameters

properties.name: count
properties.description:
Count algorithm to use to count rows returned by the
function. Only applicable for [set-returning
functions](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/functions-srf.html).

`"exact"`: Exact but slow count algorithm. Performs a `COUNT(*)` under the
hood.

`"planned"`: Approximated but fast count algorithm. Uses the Postgres
statistics under the hood.

`"estimated"`: Uses exact count for low numbers and planned count for high
numbers.

properties.name: get
properties.description:
When set to `true`, the function will be called with
read-only access mode.

properties.name: head
properties.description:
When set to `true`, `data` will not be returned.
Useful if you only need the count.

properties.name: containedBy
properties.description:
Only relevant for jsonb, array, and range columns. Match only rows where
every element appearing in `column` is contained by `value`.

properties.name: contains
properties.description:
Only relevant for jsonb, array, and range columns. Match only rows where
`column` contains every element appearing in `value`.

properties.name: filter
properties.description:
Match only rows which satisfy the filter. This is an escape hatch - you
should use the specific filter methods wherever possible.

Unlike most filters, `opearator` and `value` are used as-is and need to
follow [PostgREST
syntax](https://postgrest.org/en/stable/api.html#operators). You also need
to make sure they are properly sanitized.

properties.name: gt
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` is greater than `value`.

properties.name: gte
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` is greater than or equal to `value`.

properties.name: ilike
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches `pattern` case-insensitively.

properties.name: ilikeAllOf
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches all of `patterns` case-insensitively.

properties.name: ilikeAnyOf
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches any of `patterns` case-insensitively.

properties.name: is
properties.description:
Match only rows where `column` IS `value`.

For non-boolean columns, this is only relevant for checking if the value of
`column` is NULL by setting `value` to `null`.

For boolean columns, you can also set `value` to `true` or `false` and it
will behave the same way as `.eq()`.

properties.name: like
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches `pattern` case-sensitively.

properties.name: likeAllOf
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches all of `patterns` case-sensitively.

properties.name: likeAnyOf
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches any of `patterns` case-sensitively.

properties.name: lt
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` is less than `value`.

properties.name: lte
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` is less than or equal to `value`.

properties.name: match
properties.description:
Match only rows where each column in `query` keys is equal to its
associated value. Shorthand for multiple `.eq()`s.

properties.name: not
properties.description:
Match only rows which doesn't satisfy the filter.

Unlike most filters, `opearator` and `value` are used as-is and need to
follow [PostgREST
syntax](https://postgrest.org/en/stable/api.html#operators). You also need
to make sure they are properly sanitized.

properties.name: order
properties.description:
Order the query result by `column`.

You can call this method multiple times to order by multiple columns.

You can order referenced tables, but it only affects the ordering of the
parent table if you use `!inner` in the query.

properties.name: overlaps
properties.description:
Only relevant for array and range columns. Match only rows where
`column` and `value` have an element in common.

properties.name: rangeAdjacent
properties.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where `column` is
mutually exclusive to `range` and there can be no element between the two
ranges.

properties.name: rangeGt
properties.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where every element in
`column` is greater than any element in `range`.

properties.name: rangeGte
properties.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where every element in
`column` is either contained in `range` or greater than any element in
`range`.

properties.name: rangeLt
properties.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where every element in
`column` is less than any element in `range`.

properties.name: rangeLte
properties.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where every element in
`column` is either contained in `range` or less than any element in
`range`.

properties.name: regexIMatch
properties.description:
Match only rows where `column` matches the PostgreSQL regex `pattern`
case-insensitively (using the `~*` operator).

properties.name: regexMatch
properties.description:
Match only rows where `column` matches the PostgreSQL regex `pattern`
case-sensitively (using the `~` operator).

properties.name: textSearch
properties.description:
Only relevant for text and tsvector columns. Match only rows where
`column` matches the query string in `query`.

examples.title: Example 1
examples.code:
// For cross-schema functions where type inference fails, use overrideTypes:
const { data } = await supabase
  .schema('schema_b')
  .rpc('function_a', {})
  .overrideTypes<{ id: string; user_id: string }[]>()
examples.notes:
Bulk processing
You can process large payloads by passing in an array as an argument.

examples.title: Call a Postgres function without arguments
examples.code: const { data, error } = await supabase.rpc('hello_world')
examples.notes:
Call a Postgres function with filters
Postgres functions that return tables can also be combined with [Filters](/docs/reference/javascript/using-filters) and [Modifiers](/docs/reference/javascript/using-modifiers).

examples.title: Call a Postgres function with arguments
examples.code: const { data, error } = await supabase.rpc('echo', { say: '👋' })

examples.title: Bulk processing
examples.code: const { data, error } = await supabase.rpc('add_one_each', { arr: [1, 2, 3] })

examples.title: Call a Postgres function with filters
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .rpc('list_stored_countries')
  .eq('id', 1)
  .single()

examples.title: Call a read-only Postgres function
examples.code: const { data, error } = await supabase.rpc('hello_world', undefined, { get: true })

definitions.name: abortSignal
definitions.description: Set the AbortSignal for the fetch request.
remarks: You can use this to set a timeout for the request.

parameters.name: signal
parameters.description: The AbortSignal to use for the fetch request

examples.title: Aborting requests in-flight
examples.code:
const ac = new AbortController()

const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('very_big_table')
  .select()
  .abortSignal(ac.signal)

// Abort the request after 100 ms
setTimeout(() => ac.abort(), 100)
examples.notes:
](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/AbortController) to abort requests.
Note that  and  don't mean anything for aborted requests as the request wasn't fulfilled.

examples.title: Set a timeout
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('very_big_table')
  .select()
  .abortSignal(AbortSignal.timeout(1000 /* ms */))

definitions.name: containedBy
definitions.description:
Only relevant for jsonb, array, and range columns. Match only rows where
every element appearing in `column` is contained by `value`.

examples.title: On array columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('classes')
  .select('name')
  .containedBy('days', ['monday', 'tuesday', 'wednesday', 'friday'])
examples.sql:
create table
  classes (
    id int8 primary key,
    name text,
    days text[]
  );

insert into
  classes (id, name, days)
values
  (1, 'Chemistry', array['monday', 'friday']),
  (2, 'History', array['monday', 'wednesday', 'thursday']);
examples.notes:
On range columns
Postgres supports a number of [range
types](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/rangetypes.html). You
can filter on range columns using the string representation of range
values.

examples.title: On range columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('reservations')
  .select()
  .containedBy('during', '[2000-01-01 00:00, 2000-01-01 23:59)')
examples.sql:
create table
  reservations (
    id int8 primary key,
    room_name text,
    during tsrange
  );

insert into
  reservations (id, room_name, during)
values
  (1, 'Emerald', '[2000-01-01 13:00, 2000-01-01 15:00)'),
  (2, 'Topaz', '[2000-01-02 09:00, 2000-01-02 10:00)');

examples.title: On `jsonb` columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('users')
  .select('name')
  .containedBy('address', {})
examples.sql: `jsonb`

definitions.name: contains
definitions.description:
Only relevant for jsonb, array, and range columns. Match only rows where
`column` contains every element appearing in `value`.

examples.title: On array columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('issues')
  .select()
  .contains('tags', ['is:open', 'priority:low'])
examples.sql:
create table
  issues (
    id int8 primary key,
    title text,
    tags text[]
  );

insert into
  issues (id, title, tags)
values
  (1, 'Cache invalidation is not working', array['is:open', 'severity:high', 'priority:low']),
  (2, 'Use better names', array['is:open', 'severity:low', 'priority:medium']);
examples.notes:
On range columns
Postgres supports a number of [range
types](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/rangetypes.html). You
can filter on range columns using the string representation of range
values.

examples.title: On range columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('reservations')
  .select()
  .contains('during', '[2000-01-01 13:00, 2000-01-01 13:30)')
examples.sql:
create table
  reservations (
    id int8 primary key,
    room_name text,
    during tsrange
  );

insert into
  reservations (id, room_name, during)
values
  (1, 'Emerald', '[2000-01-01 13:00, 2000-01-01 15:00)'),
  (2, 'Topaz', '[2000-01-02 09:00, 2000-01-02 10:00)');

examples.title: On `jsonb` columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('users')
  .select('name')
  .contains('address', { postcode: 90210 })
examples.sql: `jsonb`

examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase

insert into

definitions.name: eq
definitions.description:
Match only rows where `column` is equal to `value`.

To check if the value of `column` is NULL, you should use `.is()` instead.

parameters.name: column
parameters.description: The column to filter on

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to filter with

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
  .eq('name', 'Leia')
examples.sql: `select()`

definitions.name: explain
definitions.description:
Return `data` as the EXPLAIN plan for the query.

You need to enable the
[db_plan_enabled](https://supabase.com/docs/guides/database/debugging-performance#enabling-explain)
setting before using this method.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Named parameters

properties.name: analyze
properties.description:
If `true`, the query will be executed and the
actual run time will be returned

properties.name: buffers
properties.description: If `true`, include information on buffer usage

properties.name: format
properties.description:
The format of the output, can be `"text"` (default)
or `"json"`

properties.name: settings
properties.description:
If `true`, include information on configuration
parameters that affect query planning

properties.name: verbose
properties.description:
If `true`, the query identifier will be returned
and `data` will include the output columns of the query

properties.name: wal
properties.description: If `true`, include information on WAL record generation

examples.title: Get the execution plan
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
  .explain()
examples.sql:
create table
  characters (id int8 primary key, name text);

insert into
  characters (id, name)
values
  (1, 'Luke'),
  (2, 'Leia'),
  (3, 'Han');
examples.response:
Aggregate  (cost=33.34..33.36 rows=1 width=112)
  ->  Limit  (cost=0.00..18.33 rows=1000 width=40)
        ->  Seq Scan on characters  (cost=0.00..22.00 rows=1200 width=40)
examples.notes: parameter.

examples.title: Get the execution plan with analyze and verbose
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
  .explain({analyze:true,verbose:true})
examples.sql:
create table
  characters (id int8 primary key, name text);

insert into
  characters (id, name)
values
  (1, 'Luke'),
  (2, 'Leia'),
  (3, 'Han');
examples.response:
Aggregate  (cost=33.34..33.36 rows=1 width=112) (actual time=0.041..0.041 rows=1 loops=1)
  Output: NULL::bigint, count(ROW(characters.id, characters.name)), COALESCE(json_agg(ROW(characters.id, characters.name)), '[]'::json), NULLIF(current_setting('response.headers'::text, true), ''::text), NULLIF(current_setting('response.status'::text, true), ''::text)
  ->  Limit  (cost=0.00..18.33 rows=1000 width=40) (actual time=0.005..0.006 rows=3 loops=1)
        Output: characters.id, characters.name
        ->  Seq Scan on public.characters  (cost=0.00..22.00 rows=1200 width=40) (actual time=0.004..0.005 rows=3 loops=1)
              Output: characters.id, characters.name
Query Identifier: -4730654291623321173
Planning Time: 0.407 ms
Execution Time: 0.119 ms
examples.notes: parameter.

definitions.name: filter
definitions.description:
Match only rows which satisfy the filter. This is an escape hatch - you
should use the specific filter methods wherever possible.

Unlike most filters, `opearator` and `value` are used as-is and need to
follow [PostgREST
syntax](https://postgrest.org/en/stable/api.html#operators). You also need
to make sure they are properly sanitized.
remarks:
filter() expects you to use the raw PostgREST syntax for the filter values.

```ts
.filter('id', 'in', '(5,6,7)')  // Use `()` for `in` filter
.filter('arraycol', 'cs', '{"a","b"}')  // Use `cs` for `contains()`, `{}` for array values
```

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
  .filter('name', 'in', '("Han","Yoda")')
examples.sql: `select()`

examples.title: On a referenced table
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('orchestral_sections')
  .select(`
    name,
    instruments!inner (
      name
    )
  `)
  .filter('instruments.name', 'eq', 'flute')
examples.sql:
create table
  orchestral_sections (id int8 primary key, name text);
create table
   instruments (
    id int8 primary key,
    section_id int8 not null references orchestral_sections,
    name text
  );

insert into
  orchestral_sections (id, name)
values
  (1, 'strings'),
  (2, 'woodwinds');
insert into
  instruments (id, section_id, name)
values
  (1, 2, 'flute'),
  (2, 1, 'violin');

definitions.name: ilikeAllOf

definitions.name: in
definitions.description: Match only rows where `column` is included in the `values` array.

parameters.name: column
parameters.description: The column to filter on

parameters.name: values
parameters.description: The values array to filter with

examples.title: With `select()`

const { data, error } = await supabase

definitions.name: is
definitions.description:
Match only rows where `column` IS `value`.

For non-boolean columns, this is only relevant for checking if the value of
`column` is NULL by setting `value` to `null`.

For boolean columns, you can also set `value` to `true` or `false` and it
will behave the same way as `.eq()`.

examples.title: Checking for nullness, true or false
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .is('name', null)
examples.sql:
create table
  countries (id int8 primary key, name text);

insert into
  countries (id, name)
values
  (1, 'null'),
  (2, null);
examples.notes:
filter doesn't work when filtering for .

Instead, you need to use .

definitions.name: like
definitions.description: Match only rows where `column` matches `pattern` case-sensitively.

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
  .like('name', '%Lu%')
examples.sql: `select()`

definitions.description: Match only rows where `column` matches all of `patterns` case-sensitively.

definitions.description: Match only rows where `column` matches any of `patterns` case-sensitively.

Set this to limit rows of referenced

  .from('orchestral_sections')
  .select(`
    name,
    instruments (
      name
    )
  `)
  .limit(1, { referencedTable: 'instruments' })
examples.sql:

definitions.name: match
definitions.description:
Match only rows where each column in `query` keys is equal to its
associated value. Shorthand for multiple `.eq()`s.

examples.code:

  .from('characters')
  .select('name')

definitions.name: maxAffected
definitions.description:
Set the maximum number of rows that can be affected by the query.
Only available in PostgREST v13+ and only works with PATCH and DELETE methods.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The maximum number of rows that can be affected

definitions.name: maybeSingle
definitions.description:
Return `data` as a single object instead of an array of objects.

Query result must be zero or one row (e.g. using `.limit(1)`), otherwise
this returns an error.

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
  .eq('name', 'Katniss')
  .maybeSingle()
examples.sql: `select()`

definitions.name: neq
definitions.description: Match only rows where `column` is not equal to `value`.

parameters.name: column
parameters.description: The column to filter on

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to filter with

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
  .neq('name', 'Leia')
examples.sql: `select()`

definitions.name: not
definitions.description:
Match only rows which doesn't satisfy the filter.

Unlike most filters, `opearator` and `value` are used as-is and need to
follow [PostgREST
syntax](https://postgrest.org/en/stable/api.html#operators). You also need
to make sure they are properly sanitized.
remarks:
not() expects you to use the raw PostgREST syntax for the filter values.

```ts
.not('id', 'in', '(5,6,7)')  // Use `()` for `in` filter
.not('arraycol', 'cs', '{"a","b"}')  // Use `cs` for `contains()`, `{}` for array values
```

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .not('name', 'is', null)
examples.sql: `select()`

definitions.name: or
definitions.description:
Match only rows which satisfy at least one of the filters.

Unlike most filters, `filters` is used as-is and needs to follow [PostgREST
syntax](https://postgrest.org/en/stable/api.html#operators). You also need
to make sure it's properly sanitized.

It's currently not possible to do an `.or()` filter across multiple tables.
remarks:
or() expects you to use the raw PostgREST syntax for the filter names and values.

```ts
.or('id.in.(5,6,7), arraycol.cs.{"a","b"}')  // Use `()` for `in` filter, `{}` for array values and `cs` for `contains()`.
.or('id.in.(5,6,7), arraycol.cd.{"a","b"}')  // Use `cd` for `containedBy()`
```

parameters.name: filters
parameters.description: The filters to use, following PostgREST syntax

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Named parameters

properties.name: foreignTable
properties.description: Deprecated, use `referencedTable` instead

properties.name: referencedTable
properties.description:
Set this to filter on referenced tables
instead of the parent table

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select('name')
  .or('id.eq.2,name.eq.Han')
examples.sql: `select()`

examples.title: Use `or` with `and`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select('name')
  .or('id.gt.3,and(id.eq.1,name.eq.Luke)')
examples.sql: `or`

examples.title: Use `or` on referenced tables
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('orchestral_sections')
  .select(`
    name,
    instruments!inner (
      name
    )
  `)
  .or('section_id.eq.1,name.eq.guzheng', { referencedTable: 'instruments' })
examples.sql: `or`

definitions.name: order
definitions.description:
Order the query result by `column`.

You can call this method multiple times to order by multiple columns.

You can order referenced tables, but it only affects the ordering of the
parent table if you use `!inner` in the query.

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select('id, name')
  .order('id', { ascending: false })
examples.sql: `select()`
examples.notes:
On a referenced table
Ordering with  doesn't affect the ordering of the
parent table.

examples.title: On a referenced table
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
    .from('orchestral_sections')
    .select(`
      name,
      instruments (
        name
      )
    `)
    .order('name', { referencedTable: 'instruments', ascending: false })
examples.sql:
create table
  orchestral_sections (id int8 primary key, name text);
create table
  instruments (
    id int8 primary key,
    section_id int8 not null references orchestral_sections,
    name text
  );

insert into
  orchestral_sections (id, name)
values
  (1, 'strings'),
  (2, 'woodwinds');
insert into
  instruments (id, section_id, name)
values
  (1, 1, 'harp'),
  (2, 1, 'violin');
examples.notes:
doesn't affect the ordering of the
parent table.

examples.title: Order parent table by a referenced table
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
    .from('instruments')
    .select(`
      name,
      section:orchestral_sections (
        name
      )
    `)
    .order('section(name)', { ascending: true })
examples.sql:
create table
  orchestral_sections (id int8 primary key, name text);
create table
  instruments (
    id int8 primary key,
    section_id int8 not null references orchestral_sections,
    name text
  );

insert into
  orchestral_sections (id, name)
values
  (1, 'strings'),
  (2, 'woodwinds');
insert into
  instruments (id, section_id, name)
values
  (1, 2, 'flute'),
  (2, 1, 'violin');
examples.notes:
affects the ordering of the
parent table.

definitions.name: overlaps
definitions.description:
Only relevant for array and range columns. Match only rows where
`column` and `value` have an element in common.

examples.title: On array columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('issues')
  .select('title')
  .overlaps('tags', ['is:closed', 'severity:high'])
examples.sql:
create table
  issues (
    id int8 primary key,
    title text,
    tags text[]
  );

insert into
  issues (id, title, tags)
values
  (1, 'Cache invalidation is not working', array['is:open', 'severity:high', 'priority:low']),
  (2, 'Use better names', array['is:open', 'severity:low', 'priority:medium']);
examples.notes:
On range columns
Postgres supports a number of [range
types](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/rangetypes.html). You
can filter on range columns using the string representation of range
values.

examples.title: On range columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('reservations')
  .select()
  .overlaps('during', '[2000-01-01 12:45, 2000-01-01 13:15)')
examples.sql:
create table
  reservations (
    id int8 primary key,
    room_name text,
    during tsrange
  );

insert into
  reservations (id, room_name, during)
values
  (1, 'Emerald', '[2000-01-01 13:00, 2000-01-01 15:00)'),
  (2, 'Topaz', '[2000-01-02 09:00, 2000-01-02 10:00)');

definitions.name: overrideTypes
definitions.description: Override the type of the returned `data` field in the response.

examples.title: Example 1
examples.code:
// Merge with existing types (default behavior)
const query = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ custom_field: string }>()

// Replace existing types completely
const replaceQuery = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ id: number; name: string }, { merge: false }>()

examples.title: Complete Override type of successful response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<Array<MyType>, { merge: false }>()

examples.title: Complete Override type of object response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .maybeSingle()
  .overrideTypes<MyType, { merge: false }>()
examples.response: let x: typeof data // MyType | null

examples.title: Partial Override type of successful response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<Array<{ status: "A" | "B" }>>()
examples.response: let x: typeof data // Array<CountryRowProperties & { status: "A" | "B" }>

examples.title: Partial Override type of object response
examples.code:
const { data } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .select()
  .maybeSingle()
  .overrideTypes<{ status: "A" | "B" }>()
examples.response: let x: typeof data // CountryRowProperties & { status: "A" | "B" } | null

examples.title: Example 5
examples.code:
// Merge with existing types (default behavior)
const query = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ custom_field: string }>()

// Replace existing types completely
const replaceQuery = supabase
  .from('users')
  .select()
  .overrideTypes<{ id: number; name: string }, { merge: false }>()

definitions.name: range
definitions.description:
Limit the query result by starting at an offset `from` and ending at the offset `to`.
Only records within this range are returned.
This respects the query order and if there is no order clause the range could behave unexpectedly.
The `from` and `to` values are 0-based and inclusive: `range(1, 3)` will include the second, third
and fourth rows of the query.

parameters.name: from
parameters.description: The starting index from which to limit the result

parameters.name: to
parameters.description: The last index to which to limit the result

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Named parameters

properties.name: foreignTable
properties.description:
Deprecated, use `options.referencedTable`
instead

properties.name: referencedTable
properties.description:
Set this to limit rows of referenced
tables instead of the parent table

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select('name')
  .range(0, 1)
examples.sql: `select()`

definitions.name: rangeAdjacent
definitions.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where `column` is
mutually exclusive to `range` and there can be no element between the two
ranges.

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('reservations')
  .select()
  .rangeAdjacent('during', '[2000-01-01 12:00, 2000-01-01 13:00)')
examples.sql: `select()`
examples.notes:
With 
Postgres supports a number of [range
types](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/rangetypes.html). You
can filter on range columns using the string representation of range
values.

Postgres supports a number of [range

examples.notes:
With 
Postgres supports a number of [range
types](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/rangetypes.html). You
can filter on range columns using the string representation of range
values.

definitions.name: rangeLte
definitions.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where every element in
`column` is either contained in `range` or less than any element in
`range`.

examples.title: With `select()`

  .from('reservations')

definitions.name: returns
definitions.description: Override the type of the returned `data`.
remarks: - Deprecated: use overrideTypes method instead

properties.name: order
properties.description:
Order the query result by `column`.

You can call this method multiple times to order by multiple columns.

You can order referenced tables, but it only affects the ordering of the
parent table if you use `!inner` in the query.

examples.title: Override type of successful response

definitions.name: rollback
definitions.description:
Rollback the query.

`data` will still be returned, but the query is not committed.

definitions.name: select
definitions.description:
Perform a SELECT on the query result.

By default, `.insert()`, `.update()`, `.upsert()`, and `.delete()` do not
return modified rows. By calling this method, modified rows are returned in
`data`.

parameters.name: columns
parameters.description: The columns to retrieve, separated by commas

properties.name: containedBy
properties.description:
Only relevant for jsonb, array, and range columns. Match only rows where
every element appearing in `column` is contained by `value`.

properties.name: contains
properties.description:
Only relevant for jsonb, array, and range columns. Match only rows where
`column` contains every element appearing in `value`.

properties.name: filter
properties.description:
Match only rows which satisfy the filter. This is an escape hatch - you
should use the specific filter methods wherever possible.

Unlike most filters, `opearator` and `value` are used as-is and need to
follow [PostgREST
syntax](https://postgrest.org/en/stable/api.html#operators). You also need
to make sure they are properly sanitized.

properties.name: gt
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` is greater than `value`.

properties.name: gte
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` is greater than or equal to `value`.

properties.name: ilike
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches `pattern` case-insensitively.

properties.name: ilikeAllOf
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` matches all of `patterns` case-insensitively.

properties.name: ilikeAnyOf

properties.name: is
properties.description:
Match only rows where `column` IS `value`.

For non-boolean columns, this is only relevant for checking if the value of
`column` is NULL by setting `value` to `null`.

For boolean columns, you can also set `value` to `true` or `false` and it
will behave the same way as `.eq()`.

properties.name: lte
properties.description: Match only rows where `column` is less than or equal to `value`.

properties.name: match
properties.description:
Match only rows where each column in `query` keys is equal to its
associated value. Shorthand for multiple `.eq()`s.

properties.name: not
properties.description:
Match only rows which doesn't satisfy the filter.

Unlike most filters, `opearator` and `value` are used as-is and need to
follow [PostgREST
syntax](https://postgrest.org/en/stable/api.html#operators). You also need
to make sure they are properly sanitized.

properties.name: order
properties.description:

You can call this method multiple times to order by multiple columns.

You can order referenced tables, but it only affects the ordering of the
parent table if you use `!inner` in the query.

properties.name: rangeAdjacent

mutually exclusive to `range` and there can be no element between the two

properties.name: rangeGte

examples.title: With `upsert()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .upsert({ id: 1, name: 'Han Solo' })
  .select()
examples.sql: `upsert()`

definitions.name: setHeader
definitions.description: Set an HTTP header for the request.

definitions.name: single
definitions.description:
Return `data` as a single object instead of an array of objects.

Query result must be one row (e.g. using `.limit(1)`), otherwise this
returns an error.

examples.title: With `select()`
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select('name')
  .limit(1)
  .single()
examples.sql: `select()`

definitions.name: textSearch
definitions.description:
Only relevant for text and tsvector columns. Match only rows where
`column` matches the query string in `query`.
remarks: - For more information, see [Postgres full text search](/docs/guides/database/full-text-search).

examples.title: Text search
examples.sql:
create table texts (
  id      bigint
          primary key
          generated always as identity,
  content text
);

insert into texts (content) values
    ('Four score and seven years ago'),
    ('The road goes ever on and on'),
    ('Green eggs and ham')
;
examples.notes:
Basic normalization
Uses PostgreSQL's  function.

examples.title: Basic normalization
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('quotes')
  .select('catchphrase')
  .textSearch('catchphrase', `'fat' & 'cat'`, {
    type: 'plain',
    config: 'english'
  })
examples.notes: function.

examples.title: Full normalization
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('quotes')
  .select('catchphrase')
  .textSearch('catchphrase', `'fat' & 'cat'`, {
    type: 'phrase',
    config: 'english'
  })
examples.notes: function.

examples.title: Websearch
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('quotes')
  .select('catchphrase')
  .textSearch('catchphrase', `'fat or cat'`, {
    type: 'websearch',
    config: 'english'
  })
examples.notes:
function.
This function will never raise syntax errors, which makes it possible to use raw user-supplied input for search, and can be used
with advanced operators.

- : text not inside quote marks will be converted to terms separated by & operators, as if processed by plainto_tsquery.
- : text inside quote marks will be converted to terms separated by  operators, as if processed by phraseto_tsquery.
- : the word “or” will be converted to the | operator.
- : a dash will be converted to the ! operator.

definitions.name: throwOnError
definitions.description:
If there's an error with the query, throwOnError will reject the promise by
throwing the error instead of returning it as part of a successful response.

https://github.com/supabase/supabase-js/issues/92

definitions.name: constructor
definitions.description: Creates a query builder scoped to a Postgres table or view.

properties.name: insert
properties.description:
Perform an INSERT into the table or view.

By default, inserted rows are not returned. To return it, chain the call
with `.select()`.

properties.name: upsert
properties.description:
Perform an UPSERT on the table or view. Depending on the column(s) passed
to `onConflict`, `.upsert()` allows you to perform the equivalent of
`.insert()` if a row with the corresponding `onConflict` columns doesn't
exist, or if it does exist, perform an alternative action depending on
`ignoreDuplicates`.

By default, upserted rows are not returned. To return it, chain the call
with `.select()`.

examples.title: Creating a Postgrest query builder

definitions.name: delete
definitions.description:
Perform a DELETE on the table or view.

By default, deleted rows are not returned. To return it, chain the call
with `.select()` after filters.
remarks:
- `delete()` should always be combined with [filters](/docs/reference/javascript/using-filters) to target the item(s) you wish to delete.
- If you use `delete()` with filters and you have
  [RLS](/docs/learn/auth-deep-dive/auth-row-level-security) enabled, only
  rows visible through `SELECT` policies are deleted. Note that by default
  no rows are visible, so you need at least one `SELECT`/`ALL` policy that
  makes the rows visible.
- When using `delete().in()`, specify an array of values to target multiple rows with a single query. This is particularly useful for batch deleting entries that share common criteria, such as deleting users by their IDs. Ensure that the array you provide accurately represents all records you intend to delete to avoid unintended data removal.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Named parameters

properties.name: count
properties.description:
Count algorithm to use to count deleted rows.

`"exact"`: Exact but slow count algorithm. Performs a `COUNT(*)` under the
hood.

`"planned"`: Approximated but fast count algorithm. Uses the Postgres
statistics under the hood.

`"estimated"`: Uses exact count for low numbers and planned count for high
numbers.

examples.title: Delete a single record
examples.code:
const response = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .delete()
  .eq('id', 1)

examples.title: Delete a record and return it
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .delete()
  .eq('id', 1)
  .select()

examples.title: Delete multiple records
examples.code:
const response = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .delete()
  .in('id', [1, 2, 3])

examples.title: Create a record
examples.code:
const { error } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .insert({ id: 1, name: 'Mordor' })
examples.sql:
create table
  countries (id int8 primary key, name text);
examples.notes:
Bulk create
A bulk create operation is handled in a single transaction.
If any of the inserts fail, none of the rows are inserted.

examples.title: Create a record and return it
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .insert({ id: 1, name: 'Mordor' })
  .select()
examples.sql:
create table
  countries (id int8 primary key, name text);

examples.title: Bulk create
examples.code:
const { error } = await supabase
  .from('countries')
  .insert([
    { id: 1, name: 'Mordor' },
    { id: 1, name: 'The Shire' },
  ])
examples.sql:
create table
  countries (id int8 primary key, name text);

definitions.name: select
definitions.description: Perform a SELECT query on the table or view.
remarks:
When using `count` with `.range()` or `.limit()`, the returned `count` is the total number of rows
that match your filters, not the number of rows in the current page. Use this to build pagination UI.

- By default, Supabase projects return a maximum of 1,000 rows. This setting can be changed in your project's [API settings](/dashboard/project/_/settings/api). It's recommended that you keep it low to limit the payload size of accidental or malicious requests. You can use `range()` queries to paginate through your data.
- `select()` can be combined with [Filters](/docs/reference/javascript/using-filters)
- `select()` can be combined with [Modifiers](/docs/reference/javascript/using-modifiers)
- `apikey` is a reserved keyword if you're using the [Supabase Platform](/docs/guides/platform) and [should be avoided as a column name](https://github.com/supabase/supabase/issues/5465). *

parameters.description: The columns to retrieve, separated by commas. Columns can be renamed when returned with `customName:columnName`

`"exact"`: Exact but slow count algorithm. Performs a `COUNT(*)` under the
hood.

`"planned"`: Approximated but fast count algorithm. Uses the Postgres
statistics under the hood.

`"estimated"`: Uses exact count for low numbers and planned count for high
numbers.

Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where every element in
`column` is less than any element in `range`.

properties.name: rangeLte
properties.description:
Only relevant for range columns. Match only rows where every element in
`column` is either contained in `range` or less than any element in
`range`.

properties.name: regexIMatch
properties.description:
Match only rows where `column` matches the PostgreSQL regex `pattern`
case-insensitively (using the `~*` operator).

properties.name: regexMatch
properties.description:
Match only rows where `column` matches the PostgreSQL regex `pattern`
case-sensitively (using the `~` operator).

properties.name: textSearch
properties.description:
Only relevant for text and tsvector columns. Match only rows where
`column` matches the query string in `query`.

examples.title: Getting your data
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select()
examples.sql:
create table
  characters (id int8 primary key, name text);

insert into
  characters (id, name)
values
  (1, 'Harry'),
  (2, 'Frodo'),
  (3, 'Katniss');
examples.notes:
Query referenced tables
If your database has foreign key relationships, you can query related tables too.

examples.title: Selecting specific columns
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('characters')
  .select('name')
examples.sql:
create table
  characters (id int8 primary key, name text);

insert into
  characters (id, name)
values
  (1, 'Frodo'),
  (2, 'Harry'),
  (3, 'Katniss');
examples.notes:
Query referenced tables with spaces in their names
If your table name contains spaces, you must use double quotes in the  statement to reference the table.

examples.title: Query referenced tables
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('orchestral_sections')
  .select(`
    name,
    instruments (
      name
    )
  `)
examples.sql:
create table
  orchestral_sections (id int8 primary key, name text);
create table
  instruments (
    id int8 primary key,
    section_id int8 not null references orchestral_sections,
    name text
  );

insert into
  orchestral_sections (id, name)
values
  (1, 'strings'),
  (2, 'woodwinds');
insert into
  instruments (id, section_id, name)
values
  (1, 2, 'flute'),
  (2, 1, 'violin');

examples.title: Query referenced tables with spaces in their names
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('orchestral sections')
  .select(`
    name,
    "musical instruments" (
      name
    )
  `)
examples.sql:
create table
  "orchestral sections" (id int8 primary key, name text);
create table
  "musical instruments" (
    id int8 primary key,
    section_id int8 not null references "orchestral sections",
    name text
  );

insert into
  "orchestral sections" (id, name)
values
  (1, 'strings'),
  (2, 'woodwinds');
insert into
  "musical instruments" (id, section_id, name)
values
  (1, 2, 'flute'),
  (2, 1, 'violin');
examples.notes: statement to reference the table.

examples.title: Query referenced tables through a join table
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('users')
  .select(`
    name,
    teams (
      name
    )
  `)
examples.sql:
create table
  users (
    id int8 primary key,
    name text
  );
create table
  teams (
    id int8 primary key,
    name text
  );
-- join table
create table
  users_teams (
    user_id int8 not null references users,
    team_id int8 not null references teams,
    -- both foreign keys must be part of a composite primary key
    primary key (user_id, team_id)
  );

insert into
  users (id, name)
values
  (1, 'Kiran'),
  (2, 'Evan');
insert into
  teams (id, name)
values
  (1, 'Green'),
  (2, 'Blue');
insert into
  users_teams (user_id, team_id)
values
  (1, 1),
  (1, 2),
  (2, 2);
examples.notes:
method to query the related data. The join table needs
to have the foreign keys as part of its composite primary key.

examples.title: Query the same referenced table multiple times
examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('messages')
  .select(`
    content,
    from:sender_id(name),
    to:receiver_id(name)
  `)

// To infer types, use the name of the table (in this case `users`) and
// the name of the foreign key constraint.
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('messages')
  .select(`
    content,
    from:users!messages_sender_id_fkey(name),
    to:users!messages_receiver_id_fkey(name)
  `)
examples.sql:
create table
 users (id int8 primary key, name text);

 create table
   messages (
     sender_id int8 not null references users,
     receiver_id int8 not null references users,
     content text
   );

 insert into
   users (id, name)
 values
   (1, 'Kiran'),
   (2, 'Evan');

 insert into
   messages (sender_id, receiver_id, content)
 values
   (1, 2, '👋');

examples.code:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('instruments')
  .select('name, orchestral_sections!inner(name)')
  .eq('orchestral_sections.name', 'woodwinds')
  .limit(1)

examples.code:
